data.title: Tesla: A Visionary Company Relying Too Much On Vision
data.description:
Elon Musk, the world's richest individual, cuts a controversial figure these days, primarily owing to his role as the head of the Department of Government Efficiency and his stance on various political issues, like immigration. In the eyes of his supporters, Musk is often viewed as a genius who few could match. Detractors, meanwhile, cite family wealth, luck, and the hard work of others.

With minted Ivy League credentials, getting accepted to Stanford, and most crucially, a long, potent business track record, Musk is certainly someone who can get things done. Yet, the decision to pass up on LiDAR and remove radar has made me wary of Tesla, Inc.’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) autonomous future, and even with shares selling at a steep discount, TSLA strikes me as a bad buy.

Tesla phased out radar sensors a few years ago and has never used LiDAR for commercially sold vehicles. Instead, Musk's vehicles rely on the so-called Tesla Vision, which uses only vision camera sensors, to achieve self-driving capabilities. While the system is robust, I can't help but wonder if it'll hit a ceiling, owing at least in part to its limited sensors.

Self-driving is crucial because Tesla’s high market cap and high P/E ratio push share prices well into speculative territory. It seems many investors are counting on Musk and Tesla to emerge as one of the winners, if not the winner of the full self-driving ("FSD") car race. Should Tesla fall short, say due to insufficient sensors, the downside could prove immense. Tesla's humanoid robots also promise a lot of upsides, but Tesla will face stiff and well-funded competition.

(Owing to the length of the article, I'm not diving into Tesla's storage and solar products. These are important areas of business, and storage, in particular, has been a boon for the company. Yet even with the most generous valuation of these business segments, TSLA is overvalued in my opinion).

The Blind Driving the Blind?

A video recently surfaced showing a Tesla engaged in Autopilot mode being tricked by a wall set up to look like a road. Tesla uses cameras to power its self-driving mode, but the cameras and software analyzing inputs were unable to distinguish between the road and the wall.

In the real world, Tesla owners won’t have to worry too much about walls painted to look like roads. Still, the stunt does illustrate a current weakness in camera-only sensors on automated vehicles. LiDAR and radar would have likely picked the wall up. The importance of LiDAR and radar isn't just my opinion but a widely held belief.

Elon Musk has noted that humans primarily rely on sight to drive, and thus, Tesla should be able to rely on Vision as well. However, let’s keep in mind that humans are far from perfect drivers, and one of the primary goals of self-driving cars is to create vehicles that soundly outperform us humans.

Studies so far suggest that self-driving cars are more accident-prone than human drivers. Self-driving cars were involved in 9.1 accidents per million miles driven, compared to 4.1 for human drivers. That said, self-driving platforms are evolving and improving, and for now, Tesla's system is one of the more effective self-driving platforms. Tesla claims that their self-driving vehicles suffer only one accident per 5.39 million miles driven. Still, the NHTSA has found that Tesla's self-driving mode was involved in more than 450 accidents and 14 deaths. I'd wager there would have been fewer deaths if the vehicles involved had more sensory input.

Crucially, when Tesla rolls out its self-driving cabs, the risks of accidents will increase, in my opinion. If a Tesla vehicle ends up in an accident that LiDAR or radar could have prevented, Tesla could quickly catch a lot of flak and lawmakers may become involved. In a sense, choosing to use just one sensor type results in a need for perfection for Tesla.

Admittedly, LiDAR and Radar Are No Panacea

There are downsides to LiDAR. For one, it requires an investment, although costs are coming down. While LiDAR once cost $4,000 on up, current systems can be had for $1000 or less.

LiDAR is also sensitive to weather conditions. Since LiDAR relies on light, rain and fog can prove especially disruptive. Upon contact with water, light may be knocked off course, thus potentially messing up calculations and data gathering.

According to studies, some surfaces, such as unreflective surfaces, can also hinder the LiDAR system. LiDAR can’t penetrate objects either, although that’s an issue with vision cameras as well. LiDAR also can’t read signs, which is crucial for safe roadway operations and getting to where you need to go.

Radar tends to perform better in inclement weather and offers more range than LiDAR under many circumstances. Yet radar has its own shortcomings too. Among other things, it struggles to notice smaller objects and to ID objects even if it can sense that they are present. It also can't read signs.

All of these shortcomings emphasize why redundancy is crucial. Tesla shouldn't abandon Vision but should bring as many tools to the table as possible. Ultimately, the more sensors, the better from my perspective, but yes, more sensors mean more money. With BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF) and other EV companies working on LiDAR and radar technologies, however, the risk of Tesla falling behind is real.

Robots and Robotaxi Are Tempting but Not Enough

One reason Tesla's stock has reached such a high point is that investors are buying into Musk's visions of an AI future where self-driving Teslas will put in work ferrying people and goods around. Tesla is also building humanoid robots that one day may be able to complete complex and nuanced activities once reserved for us humans.

Worldwide, the ride-sharing market size weighed in at more than $126 billion in 2024. Analysts expect the market to exceed $690 billion by 2034, expanding at a CAGR of ~18.5% from 2025 to 2034. If the market really is that big, Tesla and other self-driving car developers have plenty of room to grow and thrive. Yet if Tesla's self-driving vehicles cause serious accidents, it could quickly sink the company's efforts. I believe the lack of LiDAR sensors increases risks, and I'm not alone. Wells Fargo analysts have reached similar conclusions and are wary of Tesla's Vision only approach.

Also, Musk believes that advanced robots could be a $10 trillion dollar market. Yes, trillion with a T. Over a long enough time frame that might prove true, but if so, I'd wager it's a long way off. Analytics provider Markets and Markets has a more sober view, arguing that the market for humanoid robots right now is worth only about $2.9 billion. The firm believes the market will grow to $13.25 billion by 2029, a more than respectable growth rate of 45.5% but far from being able to justify Tesla's current price.

Musk will also face stiff competition. Boston Dynamics', owned by Hyundai Motor Company (OTCPK:HYMTF), Atlus robots already appear to be more advanced than Tesla's robots. Of course, studies would need to be conducted to determine if that's true. Further, many large companies are working on their own humanoid robots and/or investing in startups. Last year, Jeff Bezos, Nvidia, Microsoft, and OpenAI invested $675 million into Figure AI, for example. In sum total, humanoid robotics startups fetched $7.2 billion in funding, according to Crunchbase.

Some Financial and Market Data

You’ll find plenty of write-ups that dig deep into Tesla’s financials and other data sources. Still, there are a few things worth mentioning:

  • Tesla’s trailing P/E weighs in at ~123, suggesting a lot of speculation is in play.
  • The profit margin is just 7.26%, a respectable number for an automaker but low for a tech company.
  • Tesla’s revenues grew by less than 1% (YOY) in 2024,
  • Musk is an expensive leader, seeking compensation north of $50 billion.
  • Tesla’s sales in China dropped 11.5% in January 2025 (customers may have been waiting for the Model Y, however).
  • Tesla's sales in Europe have plummeted. Sales were down 49% in January 2025 compared to a year earlier.
  • BYD and other EV competition are pushing prices down. Tesla cut Model 3 and Model Y prices by between 4 to 6% in 2024.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings gives TSLA a hold rating. The valuation metric is particularly harsh, with TSLA getting a straight F. For better or worse, I have to agree with the valuation sentiment.

Here's What a Reverse DCF Analysis Tells Us

Can Tesla match the expectations implied by its current stock price? If we're talking only about automobiles, most likely not. A quick reverse DCF calculation suggests that Tesla will have to maintain a ~35% growth rate just to tread water and meet already priced-in expectations. This projection was down with the WACC set at 10%, per industry standards, and terminal growth set at 4%, matching and slightly exceeding recent global GDP growth rates.

While Tesla's shares are currently priced at ~$257, the DCF analysis suggests that the company's true value is a mere ~$77. Book value, meanwhile, only amounts to $22 per share. To be blunt, the 35% growth rate year in and year out simply isn't happening, at least not on the back of automobiles alone. TSLA's current profit margin is a mere 7.26%, and its quarterly revenue growth (YOY) totals just 2.1%.

Tesla needs its robots and self-driving cab service to take off. Otherwise, the company will fall short of expectations. Solar and energy storage can provide a boost as well, but not enough to justify TSLA's sky-high valuation. The company's ~123 Trailing P/E is also hard to swallow and no other Magnificent 7 stock has a P/E ratio exceeding 50 and most hover around 30. As a result, I see Tesla as the highest-risk Magnificent 7 stock and I don't think it offers substantially more upside.

Is a Distracted Musk a Weaker Tesla?

Currently, Musk is trying to oversee Tesla, SpaceX, X (Twitter), and DOGE at the same time. While Musk’s work ethic is famed, there are only so many hours in a day. Musk’s busy schedule could hamper his efforts to manage Tesla and may lead to suboptimal leadership (by his own high standards).

Given all the headwinds Tesla is facing, if I were an investor or on the Board of Directors, I’d want to see Musk focused on Tesla as much as possible. Risks abound, and a distracted Musk may overlook an important issue or respond lethargically to challenges.

Increasing Competition Means Increasing Headwinds

Musk’s hard-driving approach to business and leadership helped propel SpaceX into space and ensured that Tesla emerged as an early EV leader. That drive and talent could help Tesla and Musk overcome future problems, including issues with sensors.

That said, Musk may find himself on somewhat unfamiliar ground, having to compete with well-funded EV competitors. When Tesla first got its wheels rolling, it was arguably the only serious contender in the electric vehicle market. GM and others have released some electric vehicles but remained focused on traditional cars. This gave Tesla time to race ahead in the EV market.

Further, whatever your political leanings, the controversies surrounding Musk and the escalating global trade war could have a big impact on Tesla. Amid the fallout between Europe and the United States, Tesla could find the “old world” a rather inhospitable place, and Europeans may shift away from American brands. In China, the rise of BYD and other EV makers could put downward pressure on Tesla and also shrink its market share.

Too High of Risk for the Reward?

Tesla is a rather high-risk stock, with a trailing P/E ratio of ~123. Some view Tesla’s future as less focused on automobiles and more focused on Artificial Intelligence, self-driving, robotaxis, and robotics. I agree with this, but if Musk really wants to tap into the full potential, emphasizing redundancy and safety should be a priority. That makes embracing radar and LiDAR, in my opinion. Musk has laid out ambitious and grand plans for Tesla, and perhaps he can pull it off. I’m a skeptic, but given Musk’s long track record, it’d be foolish to count him out.

Also, positions and opinions can shift. If LiDAR proves necessary, Tesla can always adopt the technology with Musk's blessing. Following behind others and learning from them can help control costs. At the same time, however, Tesla may find itself behind its rivals and could struggle to catch up.

Still, increasing competition in key areas and some potential shortcomings, like not using LiDAR, could potentially derail Tesla. With the market cap already so high and so many risks mounting, TSLA remains too expensive for my taste even after the recent declines. Ultimately, TSLA is a sell for me. That said, Musk has made big things happen, and he may ultimately succeed once again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Neurocrine Biosciences: Recent Selloff Has Created A Potential Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Overview

This is my first time covering Neurocrine Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:NBIX) for Seeking Alpha. The San Diego, California-based drug developer completed its initial public offering ("IPO") all the way back in 1996 - an age for a biotech company - raising $36.75m by issuing ~3.5m shares at $10.5 per share. According to an article at the time:

Neurocrine, which now has more than $70 million, plans to use the money to help accelerate commercialization by in-licensing neuroimmunology-related products that are at least in Phase II studies; to allow the company to get early-stage programs into clinical trials before partnering them; and to broaden its research portfolio.

One of Neurocrine's programs is focused on the neuropeptidecorticotropin releasing factor (CRF), a signaling molecule that coordinates the brain, endocrine system and immune response to stress. The company said it has multiple strategies to modulate CRF, which could lead to many product opportunities.

Today, Neurocrine stock is worth ~$100 per share, meaning shares have increased in value by ~850% - a nice return for investors who brought it back in 1996. Here is how the company describes its business in its 2024 annual report/10-K submission:

Our portfolio of products includes U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved treatments for tardive dyskinesia, chorea associated with Huntington's disease, classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), and endometriosis and uterine fibroids in collaboration with AbbVie Inc. (AbbVie).

In addition, we have a diversified portfolio of multiple compounds in mid- to late-phase development across our core therapeutic areas and an expanding early-phase pipeline that includes a range of modalities including small molecules, peptides, proteins, antibodies, and gene therapy.

Ingrezza (valbenazine), a "highly selective VMAT2 inhibitor," has been approved to treat tardive dyskinesia (uncontrollable, abnormal and repetitive movements of the trunk, extremities and/or face) since 2017, and Huntington's Chorea (a hereditary progressive neurodegenerative disorder) since 2023, and earns the vast majority of Neurocrine's revenues - as we can see below:

Neurocrine has been profitable every year since 2017, earning net income of $154.5m, $249.7m, and $341.3m in 2022, 2023, and 2024 respectively, or earnings per share ("EPS") of $1.61, $2.56, and $3.4.

2024 Earnings & Guidance For 2025 Trigger Sell-Off - But Crenessity Approval Hints At Contrarian Opportunity

Guidance for 2025 is for $2.5 - $2.6bn of revenues, with GAAP R&D expenses of $960 - $1.01bn, and GAAP SG&A of $1.11bn - $1.13bn. The cash position is reported as $1.8bn in a recent investor presentation.

These results didn't meet Wall Street's expectations and Neurocrine stock fell from a value of >$150, to ~$115 per share on its "light" forward guidance, with analysts noting a 25% year-on-year revenue growth in 2024, versus a 10% forecast uplift in 2025.

Last December, however, Neurocrine secured approval for Crenessity (crinecerfont), a "corticotropin-releasing factor type 1 receptor antagonist," to treat congenital adrenal hyperplasia ("CAH") in adults and children aged 4+. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is a rare genetic condition that affects the adrenal glands.

The drug will apparently cost ~$265k per annum and analysts see it as a potential ~$1.5bn per annum peak seller. In terms of 2025, however, Neurocrine's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Matt Abernathy told analysts on the Q4 2024 earnings call:

Although not providing an annual guide, we do want to provide a few thoughts as you develop your financial models for 2025. We expect CRENESSITY to be a blockbuster medicine, helping many patients with classic CAH but early revenues are expected to be measured primarily due to three factors: first, the delayed timing of reimbursement for a new rare disease product; second, the frequency of patient flow into offices; and third, the trialing that will naturally occur as clinicians obtain real-world experience with a novel medicine like CRENESSITY.

In summary, a missed earnings target, and under-par guidance for its newly approved asset may have set up Neurocrine as a classic contrarian trade opportunity in 2025.

$2.55bn of revenues in 2025 represents a forward P/S ratio of ~4.6x, which is not especially low, but the guidance does not factor in any contribution from Crenessity, and we may also note the company is profitable and trades at a price-to-earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~28x (Source: TradingView), which is respectable, and which I suspect the company can improve on in 2025.

Pipeline Opportunities

As we can see above, there are label expansion opportunities for valbenazine in '25, and for another pipeline candidate, osavampator, in patients with an inadequate response to treatment in MDD. The earlier stage muscarinic pipeline also intrigues.

There will be Phase 3 readouts for valbenazine in both pipeline indications this year, schizophrenia and Dyskinetic Cerebral Palsy, and for NBI-’770 in MDD - a multi-billion dollar market. Importantly, the candidate met endpoints in its Phase 2 study, showing a statistically significant reduction in MADRS total score at day 28, and met secondary endpoints as well.

Given how many drugs fail in MDD, this is an encouraging sign. Two Phase 3 studies are expected to begin this year. Osavampator also met primary and key secondary endpoints in its own Phase 2 studies. MDD and Schizophrenia are multi-billion dollar markets - Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (BMY) recently approved Cobenfy (in Schizophrenia) could have double-digit billion-dollar peak revenue potential, some analysts believe, while AbbVie Inc.'s (ABBV) Vraylar earned >$3.2bn in MDD last year. While these markets are competitive, different patients respond better to different therapies, so there is room for further approvals in the space, in my view.

Looking Ahead - More Reasons To Be Cheerful Than Fearful

With its near $10bn market cap valuation, we can't necessarily call Neurocrine stock cheap, or expect upcoming data readouts to be major share price needle-moving events, as they might be for a <$500m biotech, for example, however, a quick survey of other commercial stage Pharma's driving similar revenues per annum throws up e.g., Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ALNY), >$30bn market cap, Exelixis, Inc. (EXEL), $10.2bn, BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc. (BMRN), $11.3bn, Dr. Reddy's Laboratories Limited (RDY), $11.3bn, and BioNTech SE (BNTX), >$23bn.

Comparing valuations with other Pharma's can be a dangerous game, but in my view these companies and their valuations illustrate that the recent market sell-off of Neurocrine may have been somewhat overdone. Yes, Ingrezza revenues may not be growing as fast as some analysts may have hoped, but plateauing growth feels somewhat inevitable as a drug product's revenues grow higher, and there are more label expansions in play.

The pipeline is arguably as promising as any of the above-mentioned companies bar Alnylam, with Crenessity a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) in waiting, and Neurocrine promising that it will "deliver a new medicine every two years." NBI-355, a NAV inhibitor, shares a mechanism of action with Vertex Pharmaceuticals Incorporated's (VRTX) recently approved Jounrnavx, another drug believed to have double-digit billion peak revenue potential.

We should also bear in mind that the Trump Administration's threat to impose tariffs on Pharmaceutical products imported from overseas is putting pressure on valuations sector-wide, but if they do not materialise for any reason, valuations may rise rapidly, while the Trump Administration's moves to remove the Inflation Reduction Act's "pill penalty" may ease pressure on drug pricing and give the sector a further lift.

Neurocrine hit the jackpot with Ingrezza, and one caveat is that in the case of Crenessity, lightning does not strike twice, and the drug struggles commercially, failing to replace high-dose corticosteroid monotherapies as standard-of-care. Still, if the drug contributes $500m per annum in peak sales I'd regard that as a win, and there are multiple other "shots at goal" in play.

Austedo's main competition is Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Limited's (TEVA) ~$2bn per annum selling Austedo, but this has been around since 2023 and does not seem to be impacting Ingrezza's performance.

All in all, then, I'd lean towards Neurocrine being a "Buy" today, a final reason being that I can see Neurocrine becoming a takeover target for a Big Pharma, with a price tag of at least $15 - $20bn I'd speculate. When replacing its long-term CEO Kevin, Gorman, Ph.D. last year, the company opted for its chief business development and strategy officer, Kyle Gano, Ph.D., as his replacement.

Ultimately, the value of Products + Pipeline strikes me as closer to $15bn than $10bn. Hence, I am making Neurocrine a slight contrarian "Buy."

data.title: VXF: How Does The Extended Market Compare To The Prime Benchmark?
data.description:
ETF Profile

Investors looking for a low-cost way to garner access to US stocks beyond the S&P 500 may be interested in considering the Vanguard Extended Market Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:VXF). With a listing history of over 23 years and AUM of over $100B, VXF appears one of the more established financial vehicles that covers the mid, small, and even micro-cap components of the US markets. Basically, VXF’s tracking index - the S&P Completion Index - which the former pursues through the sampling process, is the index that covers all stocks from the S&P Total Market Index (a proxy for the entire US market), besides the S&P 500.

How Is VXF Positioned Relative To VOO?

If you’re caught in two minds about whether you should pursue the S&P 500 or stocks beyond that prime bellwether, this section of the article may come in handy as we compare VXF to the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO).

Even though VXF has existed a lot longer than VOO (the latter only made it in 2010, nearly nine years after our focus product), it’s the latter that has garnered a lot more attention. Given that it tracks the chief equity benchmark (whereas VXF covers a lesser-known index), it is not surprising to note its popularity, but still, an astronomical AUM of $1322B, which is roughly 13x VXF’s AUM is something to behold.

The image below suggests that for the first eight or so years since VOO’s inception, both products received a fairly healthy chunk of flows. It was only from 2018 onwards that interest began to really shift in favor of VOO.

So, is there ample juice behind VOO’s popularity? Well, with regard to the structural qualities, VXF does lag VOO, but not by much. However, on the risk-adjusted return side, there is no comparison between the two, with the larger product coming off as the better play.

Firstly, from a cost-efficiency and stability perspective, both products are very compelling (relative to other ETFs), but VOO has the edge, with an expense ratio that is 2bps lower than VXF, and annual churn that is less than one-fifth of the churn that the latter experiences every year.

From an income angle, VOO is currently leading VXF by around 4bps, but one could argue that in recent years, it is the latter that is proving to be more rewarding, as it has grown its dividends at a far superior pace (3-year CAGR of almost 12%, as opposed to less than 8% for VOO), and its current yield is around 16bps better than its 4-year average (on the other hand, VOO’s current yield is trailing its historical average by 2bps).

Some investors may also prefer VXF’s wider reach of close to 3500 stocks which is almost 7x greater than the number of stocks that VOO covers; this expansiveness helps reduce concentration effects in the portfolio. VXF’s top 10 only accounts for 8% of the total holdings, and its major sector exposure (the financial sector) also doesn't dominate proceedings, with only an 18% weighting. Whereas in VOO’s case, 10 stocks alone account for more than a third of the total holdings, while tech stocks alone account for over 30% of the portfolio. That means in VOO’s case a lot of stocks seemingly appear to be just making up the numbers.

Unsurprisingly, given that VXF tilts more to the riskier side of the market (small and micro-caps account for almost two-thirds of VXF’s portfolio), it also has a higher rolling volatility profile relative to VOO (82% is just large and giant caps), with the current differential working out to around 737bps!

Then, even though the track record of past performance doesn’t necessarily have to persist in the future, VXF's relatively consistent underperformance (vs. VOO) does prompt a seed of doubt over its capabilities. For instance, take a look at the performance in each of the last 10 years. Only in two (2016 and 2020) out of the last 10 years has VXF managed to trump VOO, and there have been three separate years (2015, 2018 and 2022) where it has delivered negative returns, whereas VOO has only been in the red on two separate occasions (2018 and 2022). All in all, on an annual basis, VOO has delivered average returns of 14%, 300bps more than VXF. In the current year, both portfolios are down in the dumps, but VOO is once again outperforming our focus product by 620bps.

Looking at total returns alone won’t provide you with a thorough viewpoint on the risk taken to generate those returns. To better understand this, one may look at the Sharpe ratio which highlights the degree of excess returns generated (over the risk-free rate) for the total risk taken. Note that VOO has generated a Sharpe ratio that is 2.4x better than what VXF has generated. Similarly, if one only wants to measure excess return capabilities in light of the harmful volatility faced by these products one could look at the Sortino ratio; once again, VOO comes out on top with a figure that is 2.2x better than what VXF shows.

Looking ahead, those who have regard for the concept of mean-reversion in the financial markets, will appreciate that VXF offers decent odds of a bounce back relative to VOO. Note that over time, the relative strength ratio of these two products (with VOO at the base) has averaged less than 0.44x. Currently, the ratio looks oversold, trading at record lows of 0.33x, and 25% of its long-term mean, offering decent scope for normalization.

What could perhaps provide further support for VXF is that its valuations look more compelling than VOO’s holdings. Given that VXF covers a huge cohort of small and micro-caps, its earnings runway ahead is better than VOO by around 225bps. However, despite offering superior long-term earnings growth, it can be picked up at a forward P/E of 17.8x which represents a 15% discount over VOO’s corresponding multiple.

What also tilts things in favor of the small-cap oriented VXF is the growing prospect of cheaper financing conditions, with the Fed expected to cut policy rates by another 75bps in aggregate this year. A lot of stocks in VXF’s portfolio don’t have the most resilient balance sheets with a high component of floating rate debt (basically the proportion of variable rate debt for small-caps are believed to be over 3x more than large-caps), so one can see how beneficial a more dovish stance would be for them. A recent study has shown that when market expectations for the Fed’s policy stance turns more dovish, small-caps generally tend to outperform.

Closing Thoughts - What Do The Charts Suggest?

As per VXF’s short-term charts (the daily charts) the momentum very much appears to be with the bears, with the ETF now trading below all three key moving averages (the 50, 100, and 200DMA). Earlier this month, we’ve also seen the dreaded death cross pattern with the 50-day dropping below the 200DMA for the first time in around 17 months.

However, if we switch the focus to the larger time frame weekly chart, one can see that VXF is now not too far away from hitting its ascending trendline support (black line) which is close to the $140 levels. A couple of weeks back we also saw the weekly RSI drop to levels last seen in June 2022, from where there was a strong bounce in the price.

data.title: Flex: Targeting High-Growth Data Center Business
data.description:
Flex Ltd. (NASDAQ:FLEX) is a diversified international manufacturing company that does not sit still on its path to improve its top and bottom lines. My last article on the company was in January 2024, shortly after the spinoff of Nextracker Inc. (NXT), which initially caused a negative market reaction. Since then, the stock has been on a roller coaster ride, up 27.49% since my article, but down 19.72% year to date and in the last week, a recent 8% increase pushed on by a temporary pause on tech tariffs. Flex is shifting its business model from low-margin, high-volume business to higher-value business, such as its growing data center portfolio. Two recent acquisitions have led to data center revenue increasing by 45%. The AI data center market is projected to grow at a CAGR of 28.3% up to 2030, making this growing focus a potential growth driver for the company. Although, cautious that the company's revenue is still largely from more cyclical industries and the gross profit margin is lower than some of its peers. At the same time, the company is not shy of making big changes to remain competitive in a quickly changing international marketplace, rewarding long-term investors. Therefore, I remain bullish on this stock.

Company updates

In my previous article, I gave an overview of Flex and covered the major update at the time, which was the spin-off of Nextracker Inc. (NXT). The company has since acquired additional businesses such as JetCool Technologies for $42 million in cash, and Crown Technical Systems for $317 million, which both add new technologies to the company's data center solutions portfolio. JetCool is about liquid cooling for AI server racks and Crown Technical has grid-to-chip power solutions critical for hyperscaler data centers. Flex has also increased its credibility by being part of the S&P MidCap 400. From the company's website, we can get a good idea of just how diverse the company's customer base is and how extensive the services that Flex provides are.

Flex generates most of its revenue from the Americas and Asia, followed by Europe. If we narrow in on countries, we can see that 26% of the company's total revenue is generated by Mexico, followed by China, making up 17% of the company's total revenue.

The company's revenue is divided into two segments, namely Reliability and Flexibility. In the previous quarter, both the company's revenue and gross profit improved YoY.

There is a major long-term tailwind of overwhelming data storage requirements across the globe. The company is growing its data center customer base, which is benefiting from an extremely fast-growing AI data center market projected to grow at a CAGR of 28.3% up to 2030. The way Flex is taking on this AI boom is by bundling everything from rack integration to liquid cooling into sticky contracts. Since its Q3 2025, the company has announced expanding its manufacturing capacity and introducing new products. Flex has announced the release of its Q4 2025 earnings call, to be held on 7 May. Although FY 2025 revenue might not surpass the prior year, in the long term, the company looks set to benefit from industry tailwinds and its shift to higher-value business.

Financial overview

When looking at the company's top line and gross profit, it is important to understand that the company is trying to improve its gross profit margin by cutting down on low-margin business through divestments and increasing high-value business by acquisitions. We can see that the spinoff negatively impacted revenue, but gross profit TTM is the highest the company has produced in the last ten years at $2.14 billion.

The company's business moves have benefited the bottom line; we can see that net income TTM at $1.01 billion is also the highest the company has reported in the last ten years.

We can see that the cash flow has been upward trending for the last three years. This gives the company the ability to use cash to make acquisitions, which we saw earlier in the year. Furthermore, it can use the money to pay off debts or reward its shareholders.

Valuation

Over the last five years, Flex shares have increased by 264%, we can compare this to the S&P index, which increased by 117% over the same period. The company has a market cap of $11.3 billion and is trading under its average price target of $45.65 with an upside of 54.28%. The company has a 4.4 Buy rating by Wall Street analysts. If we compare Flex to some of its peers in the industry, namely Jabil (JBL), a company that can be perceived as a head-on-head competitor due to its similar size and scope, Sanmina (SANM), smaller in size and focused on high technology and communication markets, and as a third peer, we have Celestica (CLS) which is smaller but with a similar business model to Flex. If we look at the FWD price-to-earnings ratio of Flex, we can see that it is lower than its peers at 13.26, versus 18.17 for Celestica, 28.87 for Jabil and 16.55 for Sanmina. This indicates that Flex may be undervalued relative to some of its peers.

Below, we can see that the company predicts revenue for FY2025 to be between $25.4 and $25.8 billion, which would be less than FY2024. However, the company predicts multiple years of data center CAGR of around 20%, which could benefit long-term investors.

Risks

As always, every investment should be looked at with a critical eye. One of the issues that stands out with Flex is that they still make a lot of their money by offering low-margin but high-volume contracts. With a small margin, although it has improved YoY, there is always the risk that rising costs can negatively impact the profit margin. As the business has global manufacturing and sales, many elements such as the current trade tariffs or increased geopolitical tensions could negatively impact the business performance. Investors should also be aware that many of Flex's customers are in cyclical markets; although its customer base is diversified, any downturn can have a bad impact on overall sales opportunities. Lastly, the company has competition from the likes of Jabil and Sanmina which could impact future sales prospects.

Final thoughts

Since my last article in January 2024, the stock has increased in value, although the road has had its bumps and swings, up 27% over the past year but down nearly 23% year to date. Since the spinoff of Nextracker, Flex has made some serious moves into the data center market and invested in increased manufacturing capacity as well as new products. While the company is not expected to improve on its top-line performance of FY2024, there are strong signs that the management team is thinking long-term for this business. Furthermore, we're seeing improved gross profit margins and upward trending bottom-line results. Therefore, I maintain a bullish stance on Flex.

data.title: Telefonica Brasil: Market Leader, Weak Returns
data.description:
Vivo, the main brand of Telefonica Brasil (NYSE:VIV), operates throughout the Brazilian territory and is one of the market leaders in the telecommunications segment, with nearly 40% market share—41.5% in postpaid (pós-pago) mobile services, 35% in prepaid (pré-pago), and 18% in broadband internet (fibra).

The company's market dominance has led Vivo to become the tenth most valuable brand in Brazil, with its brand value growing 13% versus 2023.

Its closest competitor ranks 50th: Claro.

In terms of brand recognition, Vivo also ranks first for the 18th consecutive year, especially among households earning over ten minimum wages.

Vivo has been the most remembered mobile carrier by the public since 2003, when the category was introduced in the Top of Mind ranking. In 2024, with 33% of spontaneous mentions, Vivo stood out particularly among people with a household income above ten minimum wages and among residents of the Southeast region, says Marina Daineze, brand and communication director at Vivo.

She adds that this loyalty reflects not only service quality but also the company's positioning on broader topics, such as sponsoring the Brazilian Olympic Committee.

This effort to build a strong brand has resulted in a leading company, with over 57 million individual clients (76% of revenue) and 1.6 million corporate clients (24% of revenue).

Vivo offers a wide range of services, including voice devices (landline and mobile), mobile data, broadband, ultra broadband, pay TV, IT services, and digital services such as entertainment and cloud solutions.

Beyond traditional services, VIV has been expanding into new revenue streams like cloud, IoT, Big Data & open gateway, cybersecurity, fintech, edutech, etc. These initiatives currently represent approximately 10% of revenue and are growing rapidly.

Operational and Financial Overview

Despite its segment dominance and accomplishments in recent years, Telefonica Brasil's results haven't grown significantly—especially after discounting inflation.

Part of this performance is due to strong competition in the sector, with companies making increasingly aggressive offers and cutting costs to win over clients.

In parallel, smaller players have strengthened as the market has become more accessible through private equity and IPOs. Former competitive advantages such as concessions and heavy investment requirements are no longer as relevant. Over the last few years, smaller companies have gained share, especially in small and medium-sized towns, growing over 40% per year.

The strategy of targeting smaller cities is appealing due to less competition and lower capex. Since the IPO wave in 2021, regional players have aggressively entered the mobile line market.

As a comparison, Unifique, which until last year had no mobile operation, now serves 35 cities in southern Brazil and plans to reach 100 by year-end. The company aims to achieve 20% market share in the South by 2029 (deadline from the 5G auction held in 2021), up from 0% in 2023. Who leads in this region? Vivo (Telefonica Brasil), with 35.2%.

But VIV will face tough competition not only in the South but also in the Northeast and Central-West, where Brisanet won the 5G auction in 2021.

Although Claro and Tim will also lose share, Telefonica Brasil is expected to suffer more as the largest operator in these regions.

This disruption is most notable in mobile, but it will likely extend to broadband and pay TV, boosted by the 5G rollout.

Thus, I believe both Telefonica Brasil (VIV) and TIM (TIMB) will struggle to pass on inflation increases, and their margins will continue to fall over the coming years.

Cash Generation

With most growth-related investments completed, Telefonica Brasil has entered a phase of free cash flow generation and capital return to shareholders. That's great—but is it enough to justify holding the stock?

In my view, no. Why? Because even with a high payout ratio (around 110% on average over the past 5 years), Telefonica Brasil has not delivered strong returns to shareholders.

Will that change going forward? I find it unlikely. Given expected margin pressure and revenue challenges, how can Telefonica Brasil increase dividends if its payout ratio already exceeds 100%?

Notably, emerging players offer better growth and still manage to pay good dividends, even with lower payout percentages.

Considering growth, margins, and dividends, I see no compelling reason to classify Telefonica Brasil as a good buy. The stock appears expensive relative to the Brazilian market.

Valuation

The unfavorable market outlook and current stock price don't justify a premium valuation or even a high dividend yield (especially when compared with other companies in similar high-interest markets offering better returns).

Risks & Conclusion

As my recommendation is HOLD—with a potential downgrade to SELL if fundamentals worsen—here are the main risks to an upside scenario:

The biggest threat to the bear case is the new entrants. If they face financing issues, technical setbacks, legal problems, or other business-related risks, Telefonica Brasil could regain share and expand margins.

But since Brazil is very diverse and these small players operate regionally, a systemic issue across all regions would be required—making the risk limited.

Falling interest rates could help Telefonica Brasil, but in such a scenario, other Brazilian telecoms and the local index (EWZ) (EWZS) would likely outperform, making Telefonica Brasil a funding candidate instead. In other words, using Telefonica Brasil's shares to finance a long position (short VIV and long another stock or index, such as EWZ, for example)

To conclude, although I appreciate VIV's operations, the Brazilian market offers more attractive telecom options at the moment. I see no compelling reason to invest in VIV shares now.

Recommendation: HOLD

data.title: General Motors Must Find New Roads As Tariffs Reshape Global Trade
data.description:
General Motors Company (NYSE:GM) will be navigating a challenging market as automotive tariffs set the pace for the U.S. automotive industry. Though long-term trade policy remains unknown, what we do know is that automotive tariffs are carved out from the broader trade negotiations, potentially signaling their stickiness going forward. Despite the uncertainty in the market, GM authorized a new $2b accelerated share repurchase program in February 2025, continuing the trend from FY24.

GM is set to report Q1 ’25 earnings on April 29, 2025, with relatively mixed analyst expectations. In the last 90 days, EPS has been revised up 4x and down 3x with consensus estimating revenue to come in at $42.6b and EPS at $2.63/share. Given the current economic conditions, I am recommending GM shares with a Hold rating with a price target of $47.50 at 6.05x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

GM Operations

GM reported exceptionally strong U.S. vehicle deliveries for Q1 ’25, up 16.7% when compared to the previous year’s quarter at 693,363 in total deliveries. EV sales grew by 94% during this period, delivering 31,887 EVs in the quarter. Inventory management was a key focus for management in Q4 ’24 in order to support pricing. Vehicle inventories declined by -8% in Q1 ’25 to 549,312 units, allowing for an average transaction price of $51k.

GM entered eFY25 with an optimistic outlook for improving profitability for its EV fleet. A lot has changed since the January 28, 2025, earnings release, primarily involving the ongoing trade war across North America and China. Off the cuff, President Trump placed a 25% tariff on automobile imports coming in from Canada and Mexico. As part of the legislature, tariffs will not apply to parts and components that have been certified to being manufactured in the U.S.

Since initiating the tariffs on the automotive industry, President Trump has been exploring various exemptions for the industry in order to provide more leniency as OEMs migrate operations to the U.S. Accordingly, there are certain carve outs for vehicles that are produced with a certain amount of content in the U.S. that may be exempt from tariffs as part of the North American trade agreement. Despite this notion, the impact from tariffs could be in the range of $2,500-4,500 in additional costs for small sedans and crossovers.

China, on the other hand, may pose a greater challenge to automotive production in the U.S., particularly as it pertains to electric vehicles. China’s President Xi, in response to the imposed tariffs on the country, imposed a restriction on exports of rare earth elements [REEs] to the US, a critical material for propulsion motors manufacturing. Accordingly, China produces 69% of global REEs, potentially posing a major risk to the supply chain for the critical materials entering the U.S. This isn’t necessarily China’s first move on the matter; China had previously banned REE extraction and separation technology in December 2023. Nonetheless, there are a handful of domestic companies that own concentrated resources for these metals, including MP Materials Corp. (MP), Ramaco Resources, Inc. (METC), and Energy Fuels Inc. (UUUU), amongst others. Despite the potential trade disruption, the ban on exports may not be the worst-case scenario for the industry as prices have taken a massive turn following their peak price in February 2022.

Stronger prices for REEs could potentially improve domestic production and may benefit EV production in the long run.

One challenge GM may face as it relates to future EV sales is President Trump’s initiative regarding the dismantling of the Biden-era EV sales mandate. Though I do not believe that this will sway consumer preferences for EVs, I believe it could potentially impact GM’s profitability target for EVs. As for consumers, this could potentially remove the $7,500 tax credit received for purchasing a new EV.

As it relates to business in China, management has been focusing on rightsizing operations, reducing dealership inventories, and launching new products to cater to the market.

As for GM Cruise, management announced their pursuit of acquiring all equity in the subsidiary in Q4 ’24, executing on the deal on February 4, 2025. In addition to taking full control of Cruise, GM shifted its direction from developing a robotaxi program to focusing on full self-drive, or FSD, for customers’ vehicles with Super Cruise. As a result of this, management is anticipating a $1b annual cost-savings run rate. There is also the potential for GM to lean into licensing FSD software from Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) over time if proven successful, allowing GM to sell the capabilities without the need to develop them.

As part of its next-generation vehicle fleet, GM is deepening its partnership with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) with its Nvidia Drive AGX platform for advanced driver-assistance systems. In addition to vehicles, GM will be working with the chip designer to develop autonomous factories with AI-enabled robotics. In response to the tariffs on the automotive industry, GM is currently considering migrating certain production to the U.S., assuming the tariffs remain permanent. Assuming some operations are relocated to the U.S., I believe GM will lean heavily into autonomous factories, potentially leading to improved profitability given the possible reduction in human intervention.

GM is also nearing a deal with Hyundai Motor Company (OTCPK:HYMTF) to develop two commercial electric van models in exchange for pickup trucks to sell under the Hyundai brand in North America. The partnership involves Hyundai producing vans in the U.S. under both its own and GM’s brand by 2028.

General Motors Financial Position

Looking to eFY25, management guided a decline in wholesale ICE vehicle deliveries as they focused on balancing dealership inventory levels. This decline is expected to be offset by higher EV deliveries. Vehicle pricing in North America is expected to soften by -1 to -1.5% when compared to FY24 as a result of increased incentives and a moderation of ATPs. As a result of the Cruise restructuring, management is anticipating saving $500mm in eFY25 as they ramp down the robotaxi program.

Management is guiding a $10-11b capital outlay for eFY25 as part of their capital allocation plan. In terms of free cash flow generation, management noted that GM experienced a $3b working capital benefit in FY24 that will not be realized in eFY25, resulting in a decline in cash generation for the coming fiscal year. GM currently has $1.75b in debt coming due in 2025 and is exploring the option to refinance or extinguish this debt.

For Q1 ’25, I’m forecasting GM to generate $43.3b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $3.42/share. Management noted in the U.S. vehicle deliveries press release that the average selling price for U.S.-based vehicle sales was $51k, providing a strong price foundation.

Modeling the duration of eFY25, I believe GM may face some margin compression as a result of the 25% tariffs imposed on the automotive industry as well as the 25% tariffs on imported steel and aluminum. Given the current state of the tariff policies, I’m modeling some additional margin compression as they remain carved out from the broader 10% tariffs in place for other imported goods.

Risks Related to General Motors

Bull Case

Despite the challenges faced in the automotive market, GM is in the position to relocate some production to the U.S., softening the impact of the 25% tariff rate on imported automotive parts and components. The impact from tariffs will likely not be reflected in Q1 ’25; however, I anticipate management will address the issue in the Q1 ’25 earnings call and how they intend to mitigate associated risks. Management remains focused on managing dealership inventories to minimize dealership incentives, potentially easing the broader price decline in new vehicle sales.

Bear Case

GM has significant exposure to tariffs imposed on Canada and Mexico for imported vehicles, parts, and components, potentially leading to pricing pressure in the coming quarters. Given that automotive tariffs are carved out from the broader tariff negotiations, the automotive industry may be at risk of persistent tariffs going forward. In addition to import taxes on vehicles, GM faces the risk of higher steel prices as the 25% tariff on imports may impact steel prices in the U.S.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

GM repurchased 87mm shares in q4 ’24 for $4.68b. As of the end of Q4 ’24, GM had $300mm remaining in its share repurchase authorization. In February 2025, the GM board approved an additional $2b share repurchase authorization. In addition to this, the board approved a $0.03/share dividend increase to an annualized rate of $0.60/share.

GM shares currently trade at a discount to their peer average of 9.59x EV/EBITDA on an unadjusted basis. Though GM’s dividend yield falls below the peer average, I believe the $2.3b in available share repurchases make up in shareholder value.

Given the uncertain economic conditions and GM’s operational risks as they relate to tariffs, I am recommending GM shares with a Hold rating with a price target of $47.50/share at 6.05x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Estee Lauder Companies: Significant Pain Has Changed The Picture
data.description:
The last few years have been a disastrous time for shareholders of The Estée Lauder Companies Inc. (NYSE:EL). Back in January of 2022, I described the firm as an "excellent company," but I mentioned that shares were trading at too high a price. Little did I or most anybody else know that what would follow would be significant pain for shareholders. Due to a decline in revenue, profits, and cash flows, the company has seen its share price tumble 82.8%. This is far worse than the 14.7% increase achieved by the S&P 500 (SP500) over the same window of time.

Digging in, it seems as though there are multiple problems here. The biggest seems to be the firm's operations in Asian countries like China and South Korea. Poor consumer sentiment and perceptions about employment have played a role in altering consumer behaviors in China especially. Intense competition is also problematic. Other moves by the company that have increased its overall leverage and wasted cash that could have been allocated in more efficient ways have done shareholders no true benefit.

Today, shares of the company are trading at levels that I would consider to be attractive for such an iconic brand. But with their existing so much uncertainty, not only from an operational standpoint, but also when it comes to economic concerns, I believe the best thing that I can do is to keep the firm as a "hold" until we see some stability on the horizon.

There is an opportunity for that stability to be revealed soon. I wouldn't bank on it occurring. However, on May 1st, management will be announcing financial results covering the fiscal third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that time, analysts anticipate a rather meaningful drop in revenue, profits, and adjusted profits. If we can see the firm pull out a big surprise, I could become bullish on it. But for now, maintaining a cautiously neutral position makes the most sense to me.

Big problems

What was once a rapidly growing brand has become a problem child in the beauty and wellness space. In the chart below, you can see financial performance for the company for the last three completed fiscal years. From 2022 through 2024, revenue contracted from $17.74 billion to $15.61 billion. Dissecting the picture is complicated. This is because there are many working parts here and we need to take each of them into consideration. We should probably touch first on the firm’s Skin Care product category.

Over this three-year window of time, this part of the business has been particularly challenged. Revenue dropped from $9.90 billion to $8.25 billion. Some of this weakness has been the result of foreign currency fluctuations. As an example, from 2022 to 2023, foreign currency negatively affected this product category by $337 million. As the company increased prices, overall volumes declined. But I will get to that a bit more later in this article. By comparison, the other parts of the company fared much better. Over the last three years, The Estée Lauder Companies’ Makeup sales fell from $4.67 billion to $4.47 billion. We saw its Fragrance category of revenue remained virtually flat at $2.49 billion. Meanwhile, Hair Care sales contracted from $631 million to $629 million.

With revenue dropping, profitability for the company plummeted also. Income went from $2.39 billion to $390 million over the last three completed fiscal years. We saw operating cash flow fall from $3.04 billion to $2.36 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an even larger decline from $3.59 billion to $1.73 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business fell from $4.28 billion to $2.40 billion.

The 2025 fiscal year is not looking any better. Driven by a plunge in Skin Care sales from $3.81 billion to $3.45 billion, overall sales for the company dropped from $7.80 billion in the first half of last year to $7.37 billion in the first half of 2025. This brought with it a drop in the firm's bottom line as well. The business went from a profit of $344 million in the first half of 2024 to negative $746 million in the first half of 2025. This brings us to the first mistake that the company seems to have made.

Back in November of 2022, management announced the acquisition of a luxury brand by the name of TOM FORD. After everything was said and done, The Estée Lauder Companies ended up paying $2.58 billion for the enterprise. It was expected that this luxury fragrance and makeup business would help the enterprise to grow and to lock in long term revenue through its licensing arrangements. At the time, it was one of the top 15 fragrance brands in the U.S. and one of the top 10 in China. By the end of the second quarter of 2025, TOM FORD had underperformed enough to result in a $773 million impairment charge associated with the trademark. Clearly, The Estée Lauder Companies overpaid for it.

Because of the non-cash nature of that impairment charge, as well as the $75 million impairment charge associated with Too Faced, the cash flow figures for the business held up considerably better. For the first half of 2025, The Estée Lauder Companies generated operating cash flow of $387 million. That was down from the $937 million reported the previous year. On an adjusted basis, we get a decline from $859 million to $431 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $1.09 billion to $1.01 billion.

As you now know, the Skin Care category has been the biggest issue for The Estée Lauder Companies over the last few years. But it would be illustrative to dig deeper and figure out what is going on. Looking at geographic data for the company can be a bit challenging. Management does provide data based on three different regions. This would be the Americas, the EMEA (Europe, Middle, and Africa) region, and the Asia Pacific region. In the image above, you can see how each of these regions have performed over the last three fiscal years, while in the image below you can see the same thing for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. At first glance, it seems as though the Americas held up well from a sales perspective while the other two suffered in terms of revenue. All three took a hit from a profit perspective.

Even though this is all true, it is worth noting that one part of the company that has faced particularly difficult challenges in recent years has been its travel retail business. This is the retail side of the company that focuses on achieving sales in airports, on airlines, on cruises, and in certain downtown locations. The fact of the matter is that, over the last several years leading up to these troubles, The Estée Lauder Companies has made bank on banking on a growing and thriving global travel industry. When you consider the premium nature of its brand, this makes a great deal of sense. Those who travel are more likely to have more disposable income. So focusing a lot of its operations on a place that those people will be is a recipe for success.

Unfortunately, this is not always the case. However, before I get into that, it is worth noting that some of the regional data provided by management is a bit deceptive. I don't mean that in the perspective that management has done anything wrong. Rather, it is something that you need to read the footnotes of the company for in order to properly understand. All of its travel retail operations revenue is reported in its EMEA regional sales data. However, some of the operating income associated with that side of the business is reported in the Americas as well, the Americas seemingly not accounting for any of the sales of that part of the firm. This helps to explain why, as revenue for the Americas barely fell, its bottom line suffered as well.

Unfortunately, it appears as though none of the retail travel business revenue generated in the Asia Pacific region is actually included for the Asia Pacific operations. That is why actual revenue associated with individual Asian countries like China and South Korea is greater than the overall revenue associated with those regions in the company's financial results.

Unfortunately, management also does not provide a breakdown of revenue associated with China or South Korea when it comes to the quarterly results. But it does provide approximations by offering up a percentage of consolidated sales that those countries are responsible for on an annual basis. From 2022 to 2024, The Estée Lauder Companies saw revenue associated with its presence in China dropped from $6.03 billion to $4.06 billion. For South Korea, the decline was from $1.95 billion to $1.25 billion. Management also mentioned weakness in Japan. But we don't know what a good estimate for that would be since it accounts for less than 10% of overall sales and since management has not provided a more detailed accounting of it than that.

South Korea has some structural issues that are sure to be problematic for a firm like The Estée Lauder Companies. Due to a low birth rate, low immigration, and broader economic concerns like a high cost of living, the country has many challenges ahead for it. Already, its population is declining. Its population peaked at 51.85 million in 2020. This year, it's expected to fall to 51.69 million. And by the year 2100, it's projected to fall further to only 21.85 million.

China is facing similar challenges. Its population is also in decline after peaking at about 1.43 billion in 2021. By the year 2100, it's expected to see its population fall to 770 million. When it comes to China specifically, there have been some additional challenges as well. In 2022, the Chinese government cracked down on its citizens in order to ensure that COVID-19 could be mitigated as much as possible. From 2019 through 2022, passenger traffic for airlines fell from 585.7 million to only 249.9 million. Naturally, this would have a negative impact on any firm that is tied to the travel space. In the years since then, however, we have seen a resurgence in travel. 2024 saw the highest number of travelers on record in China, with that number hitting 664.7 million.

Even so, the damage seems to have been done. Consumer confidence levels for China plummeted in 2022 and have remained persistently low since then. This can be seen by looking at the chart above. There are other indicators that don't bode well for a company like The Estée Lauder Companies. In the table below, you can see urban depositor survey data. We have seen a meaningful contraction in both the Employment Sentiment Index and the Employment Expectation Index.

The first of these, the Employment Sentiment Index, shows a particularly nasty decline, which highlights that depositors in urban areas in China are pessimistic about the job market. In the chart below, you can also see how willing consumers are to either consume, save their money, or invest. While consumption data has remained fairly consistent, the trend has been more towards savings and less toward investment. This is indicative of an environment that is more conservative and less willing to engage in activities that could create long term growth. It also is indicative, when added to the aforementioned employment and consumer data, of a society that is less willing to spend on luxury items and that is more focused on being price conscious.

As another source indicated, the cosmetics space is relying heavily now on discounted products, especially with brands adopting a more competitive posture and as more competitors come on to those markets. That same source also pointed out how even established brands like Sephora are struggling, with its decision to lay off employees and to readjust its distribution channels throughout China. To make matters worse, there is now significant drama and uncertainty caused by the trade situation thanks to an aggressive pro tariff policy taken by the U.S. When it comes to China, goods coming from there to the U.S. will now be tariffed at 145%. The tariff rate for any American goods sent over to China is currently set at 125%. With China selling the U.S. $438.9 billion of goods last year and receiving $143.5 billion of goods from the U.S., it is certain that the situation for consumers there will only worsen.

I wish I could say that this was all the negative. But there's more. In my professional opinion, The Estée Lauder Companies has made some bad decisions in recent years. As I mentioned earlier, its purchase of TOM FORD was one of them. On top of this, however, we have the fact that the company has continued to pay out significant amounts of cash to shareholders. Admittedly, as the chart below illustrates, the amount of cash allocated toward share buybacks has dropped massively over the last few years. However, the amount paid out in the form of dividends has grown from $840 million to $947 million. This, combined with the aforementioned purchase and the declining cash flows has resulted in the company’s net debt exploding from $1.46 billion to $4.69 billion.

To give management credit, they have been working on restructuring initiatives. On February 5th of 2024, for instance, the company announced a two-year restructuring program aimed at improving the company's financial condition. Management estimated an initial reduction of between 1,800 and 3,000 jobs at the company in relation to this. That has subsequently been increased to between 5,800 and 7,000 jobs, amounting to between 9% and 11% of the company's workforce. This bout of restructuring should cost the company between $1.20 billion and $1.60 billion on a pre-tax basis. However, management believes that the long-term picture will be better as a result of these initiatives.

So far, this initiative, currently being referred to by management as Beauty Reimagined, has not yielded any noticeable benefits for the company. But that's likely because the decline in revenue, profits, and cash flows, that have all been caused by deteriorating demand have ultimately offset any benefits achieved.

Given all of these troubles, you might be perplexed as to why I am rating the company a "hold" as opposed to a "sell." To be clear, it is possible that I could end up downgrading the company, especially if the broader economic situation becomes worse. However, as the chart above illustrates, shares are still priced at levels that I would consider to be attractive. For the 2025 trading multiples, I merely annualized the results that we have seen so far for 2025.

In the table below, I then compared The Estée Lauder Companies to five similar companies. While one of the four firms had a positive trading multiple was more expensive than it is on a price to operating cash flow basis, The Estée Lauder Companies ended up being the cheapest of the group, tied with one other firm, when using the EV to EBITDA approach.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
The Estée Lauder Companies 22.1 10.8
BellRing Brands (BRBR) 77.6 22.9
Beiersdorf Aktiengesellschaft (OTCPK:BDRFY) N/A 14.2
Shiseido Company (OTCPK:SSDOY) 13.2 11.4
Coty Inc. (COTY) 8.4 10.8
Interparfums (IPAR) 18.1 11.4

There's also a possibility that I could become more optimistic as new data comes in. On May 1st, management will be announcing financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that time, however, analysts seem pretty pessimistic. Revenue is expected to come in at $3.51 billion. That would translate to a 10.9% drop compared to the $3.94 billion the business reported for the third quarter of 2024. Earnings per share have been projected to fall from $0.91 to $0.18, which would mean a decline in profits from $330 million to $65 million.

On an adjusted basis, analysts also believe that weakness should be in the cards. Last year for the third quarter, The Estée Lauder Companies generated adjusted earnings per share of $0.97. That number this year should be $0.32 according to analysts. This would mean a drop in adjusted net income from $350 million to $115 million. In the table above, you can see other profitability metrics for the third quarter of 2024. It is highly probable that these will weaken significantly as well.

Takeaway

At this time, I really want to take a contrarian approach and bet on The Estée Lauder Companies doing well. But I see no end in sight to the firm's weakness. It faces significant structural problems and economic concerns, some of which will be temporary and some of which will be permanent and worsening as time goes on, in key markets like China and South Korea. Management has made multiple bad decisions over the past few years and shareholders have suffered as a result.

Because of these factors, I was tempted to even downgrade the company to a "sell." But the fact that this is an iconic brand and the fact that shares are trading at levels that I would consider to be attractive for such a well-known business is keeping me, if barely, at a "hold."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sanmina: Fairly Re-Rated After Stabilization Arrived
data.description:
About a year ago, I was performing a balancing act in the case of Sanmina (NASDAQ:SANM). The integrated manufacturing solutions provider had improved margins and earnings, and delivered on a balanced capital return program to investors, including impressive serial buybacks.

This has created quite some long-term shareholder value, and while the near-term outlook was cloudy, the overall non-demanding valuation looked quite interesting.

Integrated Manufacturing Solutions

Sanmina describes itself as an integrated manufacturing solutions provider that provides Electronics Manufacturing Services. In essence, the company provides end-to-end manufacturing solutions across a wide range of OEM segments, being a contract manufacturer.

On a roughly $8 billion revenue base, the company generates about 80% from integrated manufacturing solutions, complemented by components, products and services. Across five key end markets, each market makes up about a fifth of sales, creating quite unique diversification. End markets include communication networks, cloud infrastructure, medical, industrial, as well as defense, aerospace and automotive.

With many of these segments benefiting from long-term demand drivers, the future of the business looks quite good, although contract manufacturers always carry a fair degree of cyclicality in their business, not to mention low (but relatively stable) margins.

A Mixed Performance

About a decade ago, Sanmina already generated about $6.5 billion in sales, as this number peaked at nearly $9 billion in the year that ended in September 2023, after which it has stabilized around $7.6 billion.

Low single-digit operating margins with the passage of time have improved to mid-single digits, as a real achievement. In the absence of steady topline sales growth, Sanmina has delivered massive buybacks. More than one in every three shares over this period of time has been bought back, resulting in real growth on a per-share basis, if we factor in modest sales advancements and margin gains.

In November 2023, the company posted very strong fiscal 2023 results, with sales up 13% to $8.93 billion, as operating profits of $455 million translated into margins equal to 5.1% of sales. Net earnings of $310 million came in at $5.18 per share, making a $58 stock look very reasonably priced.

However, the writing was on the wall, with fourth quarter sales down 8% to $2.05 billion and earnings reported at just $1.04 per share. On the bright side were huge net cash holdings, equal to $330 million, at more than $5 per share, as the company incurred some net capital investments.

The question was how soft 2024 was going to get, as the company reported a 20% fall in first quarter sales to $1.87 billion, with GAAP earnings down to $0.98 per share. The only silver lining was that second quarter sales were seen flat around $1.875 billion. While these are dirt cheap valuations, the same applied to industry peers like Jabil (JBL) and Flex Ltd. (FLEX) as well.

Nonetheless, it felt as though Sanmina, just like its peers, had become better businesses with the passage of time, which looked promising.

Moving Ahead

Since April of last year, shares have largely traded in a $60-$90 range. On a net basis, shares have shown decent gains, with shares now trading in the middle of the range at $76 per share.

This has been supported by stabilization, and the business performance accelerated toward year-end. Second quarter sales were eventually reported at $1.83 billion, and third quarter sales came in at $1.84 billion, as fourth quarter sales rose to $2.02 billion, down less than 2% year-over-year. These improved results were driven by communication networks and cloud infrastructure.

Full-year sales were down 15% to $7.57 billion as operating profits of $305 million represented margins of 4.4%, down because of the deleveraging of sales. GAAP earnings were down 28% to $222 million, as the decline in GAAP earnings per share to $3.91 per share was more limited because of share buybacks, reducing the float to 56 million and some shares. Adjusted earnings were reported at $5.28 per share, substantially higher, but as most of the discrepancy related to stock-based compensation expenses, a >$4 earnings per share number is the most I'm willing to work with.

Net cash was pretty stable at $309 million as momentum is set to continue somewhat in the first quarter, with sales seen around $1.975 billion, plus or minus $50 million. If this were to happen, the company would return to year-over-year sales growth, amidst easier comparables. GAAP earnings are seen at a midpoint of $1.08 per share, with adjusted earnings seen coming in a bit higher.

Valuation Thoughts

Trading at $76 per share, the operating asset valuation comes in at around $70 per share here. With earnings power realistically seen topping $4 per share, earnings multiples have increased to 17 times here, driven by a combination of higher share price and lower earnings.

However, the good news is that stabilization is seen and there are modest prospects for growth, of course ahead of the tariff discussions, which could be both a negative and positive. The latter might be the case as domestic producers resort to contract manufacturing to avoid tariffs, but that's just a guessing game here, as there could be real negative aspects as well.

So, while the real appeal was seen last year, I'm a bit more conservative as shares have seen quite a rally, all while the business has only really stabilized in the results. Of course, shares are by no means expensive, but amidst modest growth in the business (although growth on a per-share basis is impressive), I'm erring cautious to get too upbeat here, and for now, stay on the sidelines as the easy money has been made.

data.title: BOE: Poorly Positioned For The Current Environment, Resulting In Falling NAV
data.description:
The BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust (NYSE:BOE) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a way to access global markets and gain international exposure while still providing a very attractive level of income. This is something that could be attractive in the current market conditions, given that capital is currently fleeing out of U.S. dollar assets, which includes common equities. This is one big reason why the S&P 500 Index (SP500), which tracks U.S. large-cap stocks, is down year-to-date while many foreign equity markets are up. We can see this by comparing the year-to-date performance of the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF (ACWX), which tracks the performance of the MSCI All-Countries World Index but specifically excluding any American stock in the index, with the S&P 500 Index:

As we can see here, the iShares MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. ETF is actually up year-to-date in sharp defiance of the weakness in American markets. This clearly tells us that the current market turbulence has been largely limited to the United States, and indeed, investors who made efforts to properly diversify their portfolios internationally before the current turbulence began have seen their portfolios hold up reasonably well year-to-date. For those investors who have not yet achieved this international diversification, it could be a good time to start. The BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust may be one way to improve portfolio diversification while earning a fairly attractive 9.55% yield.

The current yield of the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust compares very well to that of the global equity indices, as shown here:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

MSCI World Index (URTH)

1.54%

MSCI All Countries World Index (ACWI)

1.89%

MSCI All Countries World ex-U.S. Index

2.82%

Some readers will likely note that the MSCI All-Countries World Index and especially the MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index have higher yields than the MSCI World Index. This is because the latter two indices have lower exposure to the low-yielding and still richly valued United States, and foreign equity markets tend to have higher yields than the American one. This is something that is not often discussed in the domestic financial media, but any investors in search of income may want to keep in mind when looking for potential investments.

The BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust obviously does better than any of the indices in the provision of income. But let us have a look at how it compares to similar closed-end funds:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust

9.55%

Virtus Dividend, Interest & Premium Strategy Fund (NFJ)

Equity-Covered Call Funds
