Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (EXG)

Equity-Covered Call Funds

10.17%

Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund (IGA)

Equity-Covered Call Funds

10.76%

XAI Madison Equity Premium Income Fund (MCN)

Equity-Covered Call Funds

13.04%

Lazard Global Total Return & Income Fund (LGI)

Equity-Global Equity

12.08%

For the purpose of constructing this chart, I tried to specifically focus on those funds that invest their assets into both domestic and foreign stocks, as the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust does. There are a number of funds, specifically several from Eaton Vance, that employ a covered call-writing strategy but only invest their assets in domestic stocks. Those funds are not really comparable to the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust in the current environment since they will actually be hurt by the capital flight out of the United States that is currently occurring. On the other hand, a global fund such as the one that is the topic of our discussion today will be both helped and hurt by this event – the U.S. stocks that it holds will continue to decline, while the foreign stocks should deliver positive performance that offsets the U.S. weakness.

Of course, the successful thesis depends on the proportion of foreign versus domestic stocks in each of these funds’ portfolios. A number of these funds are very heavily weighted to the United States, despite being global funds. This is at least partly because the American equity market has become a very high proportion of the global market capitalization:

Global Index

% of Index That Is American Stocks

MSCI World Index

71.06%

MSCI All-Countries World Index

63.72%

As the United States accounts for an outsized proportion of the total global equity valuation, many global funds have a very high proportion of their assets invested in the country, even though that particular allocation is not particularly good right now. As such, some global funds will not perform as well as we would think in an environment where U.S. stock valuations decline and foreign ones rise. In my last article on the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust was highly weighted towards the United States at the end of December, so we will want to see if it has fixed that problem today.

Recent Performance

As just mentioned, we last discussed the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust at the end of December 2024. Specifically, my last article on this fund was published on December 30, so right at the end of the year. That was right about the time that the market turned, with American mega-cap technology stocks (IYW) beginning their decline right around the start of the year:

As the technology sector accounts for a substantial percentage of American equity valuations, it dragged the entire S&P 500 Index down with it. It was not until mid-February, though, that the movement of capital out of the United States really began in earnest, which we can see by finding the point where the MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index and the S&P 500 Index started to diverge:

As such, we can expect the U.S. stocks held by the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust to have delivered a somewhat disappointing performance since our last discussion. However, any foreign stocks that the fund holds could have gained and offset some of this negative performance. As the fund was heavily weighted to U.S. stocks the last time that we discussed it, it seems likely that the fund’s performance has not been particularly impressive, but the foreign stocks still should have allowed it to beat the S&P 500 Index.

This is indeed the case, as shares of the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust have declined by 3.97% since our last discussion:

As expected, the fund outperformed the S&P 500 Index but underperformed the All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index. This is pretty much what we expected, and should the current trend continue, it will likely be the case going forward.

If we look at the fund’s total return as opposed to its price return, it looks a little better over the period:

Investors in this fund are down 1.01% since December 30, 2024. This is still disappointing, but it is certainly better than the losses suffered by most investors who owned American equities over the period. Overall, it is not really a bad showing compared to a lot of other things in the market.

About The Fund

The fund’s website states that the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust aims to provide its investors with current income and current gains. It does this by investing in a portfolio of both American and foreign equities and then writing covered calls against them.

However, it does not write covered calls against the majority of its assets like the XAI Madison Equity Premium Income Fund does. The BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust’s website states that only 43.15% of its equity holdings have covered calls against them. For the XAI Madison Equity Premium Income Fund, the same figure is 85.70%. The lower overwrite percentage of the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust means that it can benefit a bit more from any appreciation in the price of the common stocks that it holds than the sister fund, although it means that this fund will also have less premium income. This could be an attractive trade-off right now, considering that foreign stocks are continuing to appreciate and so we want to profit from these gains. The premiums received from the covered calls can also help to offset any losses on the domestic side of the portfolio, so this could be an advantage over a pure global equity fund like the Lazard Global Total Return and Income Fund.

The fund’s website makes the following claim:

Under normal circumstances, the Fund invests at least 80% of its net assets in dividend-paying equity securities and at least 40% of its assets outside of the U.S. (unless market conditions are not deemed favorable by Fund management, in which case the Fund would invest at least 30% of its assets outside of the U.S.).

This statement clearly says that the fund will normally not have more than 60% of its assets invested in the United States. However, the last time that we looked at it, it had 69.75% of its assets invested in American stocks. That suggests that management felt that, at the time, it was too risky to be invested outside of the United States. It is difficult to understand why the management team felt that though, and so far this year, the market performance has clearly shown that it was actually riskier to be invested in the United States than outside of it.

For some reason, the fund still retains a high level of exposure to the United States today. We can see this here:

The fund’s current allocation has 68.61% of its assets invested inside the United States as of March 31, 2025. That puts 31.39% of its assets outside of the United States, which is well below the 40% foreign exposure that the fund promises to have under normal market conditions. It is possible that, for some reason, the fund’s management is viewing foreign assets as too risky, but it is difficult to see why that would be the case given that foreign markets have outperformed the United States year-to-date. In fact, some of the markets shown on the chart above have substantially outperformed the S&P 500 Index year-to-date:

Country/Country ETF

YTD Performance

United Kingdom (EWU)

+8.79%

France (EWQ)

-9.62%

Taiwan (EWT)

-11.95%

Spain (EWP)

+25.64%

Switzerland (EWL)

+12.51%

Denmark (EDEN)

-3.25%

Netherlands (EWN)

+5.06%

Canada (EWC)

+1.44%

Japan (EWJ)

+0.33%

The S&P 500 Index is down 9.20% year-to-date. As we can see, every single one of these countries, except for Taiwan, has delivered a substantially better performance than the S&P 500 Index. It is therefore very difficult to see how the fund’s management can believe that “conditions are not favorable” for increasing its allocation to these countries and decreasing its allocation to the United States. If the fund had done that at any point during the first quarter, it likely would have delivered a better performance than what we actually saw.

Over the past several weeks, the financial media have been running numerous pieces suggesting that the United States may soon be in a recession. For example, Forbes states that Morgan Stanley puts the odds of a U.S. recession at 40%, Goldman Sachs puts the odds of a U.S. recession at 45%, and JPMorgan Chase puts the odds of a U.S. recession at 60%. The question, though, is whether this will be a recession in the United States alone or a global recession. I have seen reports that make both predictions over the past week or two. For example, India Times states that J.P. Morgan’s chief economist Bruce Kasman sees a 60% chance of a global recession in 2025. Peter Schiff, on the other hand, believes that the recession will be confined to the United States while the rest of the world avoids a recession.

For its part, the recent market movements appear to suggest that many investors believe that Peter Schiff’s scenario is more likely to occur. After all, if investors expected a global recession, history suggests that assets such as U.S. Treasury securities, U.S. dollars, and gold should do well. Gold has certainly been doing well, but the other two assets have not. The U.S. dollar index (DXY) is down 8.13% year-to-date:

The bond market has also not been doing particularly well, at least in the past few days. This could partially be due to the basis trade unraveling, but it is still a very unusual occurrence in an environment in which investors fear a global recession. However, it might make sense in an environment where the United States alone experiences a recession while the rest of the world remains relatively unscathed. In that environment, assets (including equities) backed by issuers in countries with strong economies would still be perceived as reasonable things to hold. The assets to avoid would be American ones, just like what happens when a single-country recession has occurred in any nation around the world during any other period of time. Investors pull assets out of nations with weakening economies and put them into assets from countries with relative economic strength. This could be what we are seeing in the current market capital flows.

The current positioning of the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust is not ideal for either recessionary scenario. In the event of a global recession, the equity issued by most companies all over the world would decline and naturally have an adverse impact on the price of the shares of this fund. That does not seem to be the scenario that the market currently envisions, though. Rather, the market seems to expect that the United States alone will experience a recession while the rest of the world does fine. In that scenario, we would want to have an allocation that is underweight to the United States and overweight to the rest of the world. This fund is doing the opposite, which is not ideal. While it is possible that it will change its portfolio to one that favors foreign assets going forward, it should have done that already given the market moves that have already occurred since the start of this year. It has not done that, and indeed, its own strategy description suggests that management is worried about the rest of the world given its current allocation.

Dividend Analysis

The BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust currently pays a monthly distribution of $0.0827 per share ($0.9924 per share annually), which gives it a 9.55% yield at the current share price. As we saw in the introduction, the fund’s current yield is a bit lower than that of its peers. This is not unusual for a BlackRock fund, though, as they frequently have lower yields than many other similar funds in the market (along with higher valuations compared to their net asset values).

The fund’s most recent financial report is its annual report for the full-year period that ended on December 31, 2024. This report shows that the fund managed to cover its distribution for the period, but it had to rely on unrealized capital gains to do it. The fund’s net investment income plus net realized gains totaled $31,890,148, which was less than the $47,183,628 that it paid out. If we add in the unrealized gains, though, we get $62,397,807 net change in assets from operations for the period, which was sufficient to cover the distribution.

As might be expected given its overexposure to the United States in the current market, the fund has failed to cover its distributions year-to-date. As we can see here, its net asset value has fallen by 6.88% year-to-date:

If a fund’s net asset value per share declines during any given period, it means that the fund has not been generating sufficient investment profits to cover the money that it is paying out. We can clearly see that this is the case with this fund. If the current market trends continue and capital continues to flow out of the United States and into foreign markets, it seems likely that this fund will have to cut its distribution at some point.

Valuation

Shares of the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust are currently trading at a 9.59% discount on net asset value. This compares favorably to the 8.36% average discount that the fund has had over the past month.

Over the past three years, the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust has traded at discounts ranging from 7.69% to 16.67% to net asset value. The average price over the period was a 12.34% discount, which is a much more attractive price than what the fund has right now.

Here is how the fund’s current valuation compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust

(9.59%)

Virtus Dividend, Interest & Premium Strategy Fund

(12.36%)

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund

(9.21%)

Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund

(4.04%)

XAI Madison Equity Premium Income Fund

(3.99%)

Lazard Global Total Return & Income Fund

(9.89%)

The current price of the BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust seems reasonable when compared to its peers. However, potential investors should still keep in mind that its portfolio is overly exposed to the United States, which may not be the best country to be overweight right now, and it is more expensive than its three-year average price.

Expenses

The BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust has an expense ratio of 1.11%:

This is an increase compared to what the fund had the last time that we discussed it, which was likely due to its falling net asset value.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the last several weeks have seen capital flow out of the United States and into equity markets around the world. This could be a sign that the market expects the United States to enter into a recession while the rest of the world continues to deliver economic growth. Naturally, the way to profit from this trend is to increase exposure to foreign markets while decreasing American exposure. This fund is poorly positioned for the current environment given its very high exposure to American markets. This has cost it, as the fund’s net asset value has been declining since the start of the year and likely jeopardizing the distribution.
